Typical celebration time (Gregorian): Oct–Nov (most common; 4 days, ~1 week after Diwali)
Where in Bihar (notable): Across Bihar; especially Patna, Vaishali, Begusarai, Bhojpur, Darbhanga, etc.
How it’s celebrated (key details): 4-day vrat; ‘Nahay-Khay’, ‘Kharna’, evening arghya to setting Sun, morning arghya to rising Sun at rivers/ponds; offerings in soop (bamboo basket), community ghats
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Deep pride as Bihar’s signature festival; devotion + discipline; family/community bonding at ghats; gratitude to Sun and nature

How it’s celebrated (key details): Same 4-day structure as Kartik Chhath; smaller but equally strict observance
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Seen as a ‘second Chhath’ for vows/thanks; often done for family health or a wish-fulfilled mannāt

Lunar timing (common): Starts after Kartik Purnima; ends around full moon (varies by region)
Where in Bihar (notable): Mithila region: Darbhanga, Madhubani, Sitamarhi, Samastipur
How it’s celebrated (key details): Clay/bamboo idols of Sama & Chakeva, folk songs, nightly gatherings by young women, symbolic rituals; celebrates sibling bond and seasonal migration folklore
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Strong Maithil cultural identity; sisters pray for brothers’ well-being; keeps folk art + songs alive

Festival: Sonepur Mela (Harihar Kshetra Mela / cattle fair)
Lunar timing (common): Begins around Kartik Purnima (full moon after Diwali)
How it’s celebrated (key details): Huge fair: livestock trade, crafts, folk performances, rides; pilgrims take ritual bath and visit Harihar Nath temple
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Pride in Bihar’s famed fair; mix of faith + commerce + rural recreation; a ‘big annual outing’ for families

How it’s celebrated (key details): Devotees collect Ganga jal, walk as Kanwariyas, offer to Shiva; bazaars, camps, devotional music
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Seen as intense bhakti + endurance; community service (seva) through camps/food

Lunar timing (common): Pitru Paksha (Krishna Paksha of Bhadrapada/early Ashwin)
How it’s celebrated (key details): Families perform pind-daan and shraddha rituals at ghats and sacred sites
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Deep ancestral duty; many travel from far; belief in peace/moksha for ancestors

How it’s celebrated (key details): Multi-day cultural programs: folk/classical music & dance, crafts/food stalls, tourism events
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Regional pride + tourism; celebrates Bihar’s arts and heritage in a modern festival format

Festival: Malmas Mela (Adhika Maas fair at Rajgir)
Typical celebration time (Gregorian): Occurs only in extra lunar month years (every ~2–3 years)
How it’s celebrated (key details): Pilgrimage bathing, temple visits, fairs and religious discourses during the extra month
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Seen as rare, highly auspicious; major influx of pilgrims; local economy + devotion both rise

Where in Bihar (notable): Bodh Gaya (Gaya), Rajgir, Vaishali, Nalanda sites
How it’s celebrated (key details): Prayer meetings, processions, meditation, lamps; monasteries host ceremonies for Buddha’s birth/enlightenment/parinirvana
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Bihar’s global Buddhist connection; harmony + spiritual tourism; locals value it as heritage

How it’s celebrated (key details): Cultural shows, exhibitions, heritage tourism programming (varies by year)
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Pride in ancient Vaishali history; blends culture + tourism + local identity

Lunar timing (common): Ashtami of Krishna Paksha (Ashwin/Bhadrapada, regional variation)
How it’s celebrated (key details): Mothers fast (often nirjala) for children’s long life; puja, story-listening, community gatherings
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Strong maternal devotion; emotional family focus on child well-being

Lunar timing (common): Often linked to Shravan month (regional tradition)
How it’s celebrated (key details): Newly married women perform multi-day rituals, snake/serpent worship elements, folk art/ritual storytelling
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Symbol of Maithil marriage traditions; blessing for marital happiness + protection

How it’s celebrated (key details): Worship of goddess Manasa; Manjusha folk art displays; folk tales of Bihula
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Local cultural pride; faith for protection from snakebite/illness; preserves Manjusha art

How it’s celebrated (key details): Holy dips, charity, kite-flying in places, seasonal foods like dahi-chura, tilkut, laddoos, khichdi
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Harvest/seasonal gratitude; ‘simple but big’ home festival tied to food and community

How it’s celebrated (key details): Holika dahan, colors, songs, sweets; local folk styles (phagua)
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Community joy + social mixing; villages often treat it as major cultural release after winter

How it’s celebrated (key details): Lakshmi-Ganesha puja, lamps, sweets, family visits; local markets peak
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Family prosperity festival; major economic + social milestone of the year

How it’s celebrated (key details): Pandals, aarti, cultural nights, immersion; Ramleela in many towns
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Big public celebration; community organization and cultural pride through pandals

How it’s celebrated (key details): Saraswati idol worship, books/instruments placed for blessings, immersion next day
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Youth-centric festival; identity around learning, arts, exams, and new beginnings

How it’s celebrated (key details): Processions, bhajans, akharas in some places; temple visits
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Devotional + community procession culture; reinforces local religious networks

How it’s celebrated (key details): Holy bath, temple visits; often coincides with fairs like Sonepur Mela
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Pilgrimage + faith; for many it’s an annual ‘must-do’ sacred bath day

How it’s celebrated (key details): Moon-sighting rituals, offerings; linked with Ganesh traditions and local Maithil customs
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Cultural marker for Mithila households; family protection/auspiciousness theme

How it’s celebrated (key details): Worship of Karam tree branch, dance, songs, community night rituals
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Community solidarity festival; strong among Adivasi/OBC rural groups as identity + harvest well-being

How it’s celebrated (key details): Namaz, charity (zakat fitr), family feasts, new clothes
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Major communal celebration; reinforces kinship, charity, and social harmony

How it’s celebrated (key details): Namaz, qurbani, sharing meat with family/poor
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Faith + sacrifice + charity; community-sharing focus

How it’s celebrated (key details): Volunteers clean ghats, manage lighting/queues, distribute water/first-aid
How people relate to it (meaning/identit: Civic pride; public service seen as punya (merit) and community responsibility
